did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used an AI-based image classification system named DEXI, which is built on the EfficientNetV2 convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, to classify skin lesions and compare its diagnostic accuracy against an expert dermatologist and a non-expert.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The study presents original experimental results from a reader study comparing the diagnostic accuracy of an AI-based image classification system (DEXI) against an expert dermatologist and a non-expert on a specific dataset of 1047 dermoscopic images. It includes original methods, results (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy metrics), and conclusions derived from this new data collection and analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Comparison of Skin Lesions’ Diagnoses Between AI-Based Image Classification, an Expert Dermatologist, and a Non-Expert
brief_summary: This study evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of an AI-based image classification system (DEXI), an expert dermatologist, and a non-expert medical student in classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant. The assessment utilized a dataset of 1047 dermoscopic images from 302 histologically confirmed lesions collected at the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia. The AI model, built on the EfficientNetV2 architecture, was compared against human classifiers using metrics including sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1 score. The results showed that DEXI achieved high sensitivity (93.59%) and notably higher specificity (70.42%) compared to both human experts, significantly outperforming them across all core diagnostic measures. While the expert dermatologist excelled in diagnosing melanoma, the AI demonstrated a more balanced overall performance with fewer false positives. The study concludes that AI-based systems can effectively augment clinical expertise but are not yet a replacement for expert judgment, highlighting the need for continued validation and dataset diversity.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign melanocytic lesion; 180 cases in the study dataset; classified as benign in ground truth.
Malignant melanocytic lesion; 54 cases in the study dataset; classified as malignant in ground truth.
Malignant non-melanocytic lesion; 21 cases in the study dataset; classified as malignant in ground truth.
Malignant non-melanocytic lesion; 3 cases in the study dataset; classified as malignant in ground truth.
Precancerous lesion; 3 cases in the study dataset; classified as benign in ground truth for this study despite clinical precancerous status.
Benign non-melanocytic lesion; 26 cases in the study dataset; classified as benign in ground truth.
Benign lesion; 4 cases in the study dataset; classified as benign in ground truth.
Lesions of unknown type; 11 cases in the study dataset; excluded from specific benign/malignant ground truth assignment in Table 2 but included in the dataset.

Listed in Table 1 (ISIC archive diagnostic lesions) as a category DEXI was trained on, but not explicitly listed in the study dataset composition (Chunk 17) or ground truth table (Table 2) for the 302 lesions analyzed.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of skin lesions as benign or malignant, and multi-class diagnosis of specific lesion types (e.g., Nevus, Melanoma, BCC, SCC, etc.).
all_task_types.details: The AI model (DEXI) performs image classification to categorize skin lesions. The study evaluates its performance in binary classification (benign vs. malignant) using a malignancy risk score threshold (≥3.5) derived from the model's output probabilities. The model is also trained to distinguish between eight specific lesion categories (melanoma, BCC, SCC, Nevus, etc.) as listed in Table 1. The primary evaluation metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, F1 score) are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: University of Modena and Reggio Emilia Dermatology Department Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of dermoscopic images collected at the Dermatology Department of the University of Modena and Reggio Emilia. It consists of images from 302 unique skin lesions with histopathological confirmation, selected based on clinical suspicion of malignancy to reflect real-world diagnostic conditions. The dataset represents an Italian population. While the full dataset is not publicly released due to ongoing research constraints, it is available for academic collaboration under restricted access.

use.testing: Used as the primary dataset for the reader study to evaluate and compare the diagnostic accuracy of the AI system (DEXI), an expert dermatologist, and a non-expert. Images were randomly selected and independently evaluated by the classifiers blind to the final histopathological diagnosis.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image archive used for training the DEXI AI model. It contains 81,155 images labeled with diagnostic classifications and benign/malignant status.

use.training: Used as part of the training data for the DEXI AI model (along with proprietary datasets from Canfield Scientific).
use.other: The non-expert medical student underwent guided exploration of the ISIC image archive as part of their targeted training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure inter-rater agreement between the AI system (DEXI), the expert dermatologist, and the non-expert medical student.
To evaluate the discriminative ability of the AI model (DEXI) in distinguishing malignant from benign lesions.
To provide uncertainty estimates for the AUC of the AI model and the Cohen’s kappa values for inter-rater agreement.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in diagnostic performance metrics (precision, recall, F1 score) between the compared classifiers (e.g., DEXI vs. Expert, DEXI vs. Non-Expert, Expert vs. Non-Expert).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in diagnostic performance metrics between the compared classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical analysis using exact permutation testing confirmed significant performance differences. DEXI significantly outperformed the expert in precision (p=0.0229), recall (p<0.001), and F1 score (p<0.001). DEXI also significantly outperformed the non-expert in all metrics (p<0.001). Significant differences were also found between the expert and non-expert in precision (p=0.0270), recall (p<0.001), and F1 score (p=0.0240).

University of Modena and Reggio Emilia D.evaluation_setting: Binary classification (Benign vs. Malignant) and Multi-class diagnosis by lesion type
University of Modena and Reggio Emilia D.notes: DEXI significantly outperformed both human classifiers in precision, recall, and F1 score. The expert excelled in diagnosing Melanoma (94.44%) and BCC (80.95%). DEXI showed high sensitivity (0.936) and notably higher specificity (0.704) compared to the expert (0.328) and non-expert (0.286). Performance for all classifiers declined on Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL).

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained using a combination of proprietary datasets from Canfield Scientific and publicly available dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive (81,155 images).

training_details.dataset_source: Proprietary Canfield Scientific datasets and ISIC archive

classification_or_output_head.threshold: Score ≥3.5 used to classify as malignant

conclusions: AI-based image classification systems (specifically DEXI) can serve as a valuable tool in dermatological diagnostics, potentially augmenting the capabilities of dermatologists.
AI-based systems are not yet a replacement for expert clinical judgment.
Continued improvements and validation in diverse clinical settings are necessary before widespread implementation of AI in dermatology.

The AI model demonstrated high sensitivity (93.59%) and notably higher specificity (70.42%) compared to both an expert dermatologist (32.86%) and a non-expert (28.64%), indicating a superior ability to minimize false positives.
While the AI performed well overall, the expert dermatologist excelled in diagnosing specific cases like melanoma (94.44% accuracy) compared to the AI (85.18%).

The diagnostic strategy of the AI differs from that of human evaluators, as indicated by lower inter-rater agreement (Cohen’s kappa) between AI and humans compared to between humans, highlighting the complementary nature of human expertise and AI-driven classification.
Performance for all classifiers declined on Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL), indicating challenges with these specific lesion types.
important_remarks: The AI model (DEXI) is proprietary and not yet approved for clinical use, limiting immediate deployment relevance.

The study dataset consists of 302 lesions selected based on clinical suspicion of malignancy from a high-risk Italian population, which may not represent the general population's mole distribution and limits external validity.
The dataset primarily includes Fitzpatrick skin types I–III, raising concerns about algorithmic bias and generalizability to darker skin types.

Performance for all classifiers declined significantly on Actinic Keratosis (AK) and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL), suggesting underrepresentation of these types in training data or intrinsic diagnostic difficulty.
The study relied solely on 2D dermoscopic images, potentially limiting real-world applicability compared to clinical assessments using 3D or dynamic imaging.
The non-expert classifier received only 3 months of targeted, informal training without a standardized curriculum, which may limit the generalizability of the human baseline comparison.
The full dataset is not publicly released due to ongoing research constraints, preventing independent replication and external validation.
The AI model achieved high sensitivity (93.59%) but lower specificity (70.42%) compared to its performance on other metrics, indicating a trade-off in false positive rates.
